fields.description:
# ksqlDB Error Investigation -- Unified Incident Report

**Generated:** 2026-06-01T14:33:31.857Z

## Executive Summary

The ksqlDB errors are caused by a 90-minute Kafka controller election storm on cluster shared-services-msk-example.

comments.body:
<custom data-type="mention" data-id="id-0">@Ops Engineer</custom>

Incident Report: Persistent Confluent Kafka Bootstrap Broker Disconnections -- example-consumer-service

## Executive Summary

The example-consumer-service consumer groups have been continuously failing to complete the Kafka bootstrap handshake against the Confluent Cloud broker lkc-example1.dom0example.eu-central-1.aws.confluent.cloud:9092 for 30 days. The broker node ID remains id: -1 on every attempt. The root cause is a Confluent Cloud-layer SASL/TLS authentication failure. The most probable immediate trigger is that the Confluent API key or secret stored in SSM is invalid. A DNS failure would produce Unknown host, not terminated during authentication.

Confidence: 0.87

Root cause found: it's a DNS/network gap, not credentials.

example-consumer-service connects directly to Confluent Cloud at lkc-example1.dom0example...:9092. From the workload VPC (vpc-0example1234567890a) that hostname resolves to non-routable Confluent-internal IPs (10.1.x.x), because the VPC has no Confluent Route53 resolver rule associated. Resolver associations are per-VPC and not transitive over the TGW.

The legacy bootstrap-servers env var pointing at shared-services-msk-example is vestigial. The empty MSK consumer groups are orphans from that retired path. MSK is not in the incident path.

Fix: associate resolver rule rslvr-rr-0example000000001 (dom0example) with vpc-0example1234567890a, codified in the infrastructure repo.

content.text: Devs confirmed this was parked and never fully tested so the fix I suggested should finish infra part and connection to CC should be possible from the workload accounts
